questions.question: What does 'reduction tightness' refer to in security proofs?

options.text: How small the key sizes are
How much security is lost in the proof reduction

questions.explanation: Reduction tightness measures how much security advantage is 'lost' when reducing the scheme's security to an underlying hard problem. Tight reductions (ε' ≈ ε) are preferred over loose reductions.

questions.question: What hard problem does ML-KEM's security reduce to?
questions.explanation: ML-KEM's security is based on the Module-LWE (Learning With Errors) problem, a lattice-based hard problem believed to be resistant to quantum attacks.

questions.question: Why was SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+) selected despite having large signature sizes?

options.text: It had the smallest public keys
It provides mathematical diversity with hash-only security
It was the only signature scheme submitted

questions.explanation: SLH-DSA was selected because it provides mathematical diversity - its security relies only on hash function properties, offering insurance if lattice-based schemes face unexpected attacks.

questions.question: What implementation challenge delayed FN-DSA (FALCON) standardization?

options.text: Floating-point arithmetic and Gaussian sampling complexity

questions.explanation: FALCON requires either floating-point or careful fixed-point arithmetic and Gaussian sampling, which are tricky to implement securely, leading to delayed standardization.

questions.question: Why was Kyber selected over NTRU for the KEM standard?

options.text: Kyber had much smaller key sizes
Kyber had better overall balance: simpler spec, tighter proofs, best performance

questions.explanation: Kyber was selected for best overall balance - simpler specification, tighter security proofs, and best performance among finalists. NTRU had similar security but more complex specification and patent history.

questions.question: What type of side-channel attack requires constant-time implementation?
questions.explanation: Timing attacks exploit variations in execution time based on secret data. Constant-time implementation ensures operations take the same time regardless of the secret values being processed.

questions.question: Security proofs alone are sufficient to guarantee an algorithm is secure, as demonstrated by the NIST PQC competition.
questions.explanation: The SIKE break demonstrated that security proofs are necessary but not sufficient. SIKE had formal security proofs but was still completely broken by a classical attack, showing that proofs + extensive cryptanalysis are required.
